quick_hits: The leathery red fruit is packed with hundreds of juicy seeds, each wrapped in its own jewel-like sac of edible pulp.
It is one of the oldest cultivated fruits on Earth — grown and prized for more than 5,000 years.
The many-seeded fruit has long been a symbol of abundance and fertility across cultures from Persia to the Mediterranean.
The word "grenade" comes from the pomegranate — early grenades were named for the fruit they resembled, seeds and all.
species.origin: Native to a region stretching from Iran (Persia) through northern India and into Central Asia. Cultivated since antiquity, it spread across the Mediterranean, the Middle East, and eventually the warm Americas. It grows well in Florida's warm climate, though it fruits best where summers are drier.
more_information: The pomegranate is among the most ancient of cultivated fruits, with a history reaching back more than five thousand years across Persia, the Mediterranean, and South Asia. Its many seeds made it a near-universal symbol of abundance, fertility, and renewal, woven into mythology, art, and religious texts.

Each fruit is a tidy piece of engineering. Inside the tough rind, hundreds of seeds are each surrounded by a translucent sac of tart-sweet juice called an aril — the part you actually eat — separated by bitter white membranes.
There is a language footnote hiding in the fruit. The pomegranate's old French name, "pomme grenade" (seeded apple), gave us the word grenade for the seed-filled explosive it was thought to resemble.
wildlife_value: The bright tubular flowers are rich in nectar and draw bees, butterflies, and occasionally hummingbirds. Ripe and split fruit feeds birds and small mammals. The dense, twiggy shrub also offers nesting and shelter cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A deciduous large shrub or small tree, often multi-trunked, with thin branches and sometimes thorny twigs.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Showy, waxy, orange-red, trumpet-shaped blooms in late spring and summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, leathery-skinned, yellow-brown to deep red, crowned with the dried flower calyx; packed with seeds in juicy red arils.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Narrow, glossy, opposite, bronze-tinted when young, turning yellow before dropping.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A twiggy shrub with vivid orange-red flowers in summer and round, crown-tipped fruit later in the season. The little "crown" on top of the fruit is the giveaway.

size.habit: multi-trunked shrub or small tree, often suckering

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: fruits best in hot, drier summers; Florida humidity can reduce fruit quality

edibility.detail: Edible. The juicy arils around each seed are eaten fresh or juiced; the tart rind and membranes are not.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people in normal use. The fruit is a common food.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The fruit may cause mild stomach upset if a dog eats a large amount, especially the rind.

quick_hits: Slice the fruit crosswise and every piece comes out a perfect five-pointed star.
It is delicious and refreshing — but people with kidney disease should never eat it.
Starfruit carries a natural neurotoxin that healthy kidneys filter out harmlessly, but damaged kidneys cannot, which can cause serious illness.
The tree fruits heavily, sometimes producing hundreds of pounds of star-shaped fruit in a single year.
species.origin: Native to tropical Southeast Asia, likely originating in the Malay Peninsula or Indonesia. It has been cultivated across the tropics for centuries and grows well in warm, frost-free parts of Florida.
more_information: Carambola, better known as starfruit, is grown as much for its looks as its flavor — sliced crosswise, it yields flawless edible stars that turn an ordinary fruit salad into a centerpiece. The fruit is crisp, juicy, and ranges from tart to sweet depending on variety and ripeness.

Behind the cheerful shape sits a genuine medical caution. Starfruit contains a neurotoxin called caramboxin, plus oxalic acid. Healthy kidneys clear these compounds without trouble, but people with kidney disease cannot — and even a small amount can cause hiccups, confusion, seizures, and in serious cases death. For most people it is a harmless treat; for dialysis and kidney patients it is genuinely dangerous, which is worth knowing.
The tree is a generous producer, flowering and fruiting through much of the warm season and often carrying flowers and fruit at the same time.
wildlife_value: The small lilac flowers attract bees and other pollinators, and ripe or fallen fruit feeds birds and small mammals. As a dense evergreen, it offers good cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A small, bushy evergreen tree with a rounded, often drooping canopy.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, lilac to pink, in clusters on branches and twigs, sometimes straight from the trunk.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Waxy, yellow-green to golden, with five (sometimes six) prominent ribs; star-shaped in cross-section.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnately compound with soft oval leaflets that fold down at night.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A small tree hung with ribbed, waxy yellow fruit. Slice one across to reveal the star — the unmistakable signature of carambola.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive — protect from hard freezes

edibility.detail: Edible and popular eaten fresh, but see the safety note: people with kidney disease should avoid it entirely.

toxicity.people: Edible for most people, but dangerous for anyone with kidney disease. Starfruit contains caramboxin (a neurotoxin) and oxalates that healthy kidneys filter out but impaired kidneys cannot, which can cause serious, even fatal, reactions.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution with pets. The fruit's soluble oxalates can affect a dog's kidneys, especially in quantity; keep fallen fruit picked up.

alternate_names: Golden Rain Tree (one of several plants by that name)
quick_hits: In late spring it erupts into long, hanging chains of brilliant golden-yellow flowers, like a tree dripping in sunshine.
It is the national tree and flower of Thailand, where its blooms symbolize royalty and good fortune.
The long, dark seed pods that follow can reach two feet and rattle their seeds in the wind.
The sticky pulp inside those pods is a traditional, and very effective, natural laxative.
species.origin: Native to South and Southeast Asia, from India and Sri Lanka through Myanmar and Thailand. Long cultivated as an ornamental across the tropics for its spectacular bloom, and grown in frost-free parts of Florida.
more_information: The golden shower tree earns its name once a year, when it covers itself in cascading clusters of bright yellow flowers so dense they can nearly hide the foliage. The display is brief but unforgettable, and has made the tree a celebrated ornamental and the national symbol of Thailand.

After flowering come the pods — long, narrow, cylindrical, and nearly black when ripe, sometimes approaching two feet in length, with seeds set in dark, sticky pulp between papery partitions. That pulp (cassia pulp) has a long history in traditional medicine as a gentle but reliable laxative.
It belongs to the pea family, and the flush-then-pod rhythm is a legume signature, even on a tree this showy.
wildlife_value: The abundant nectar-rich flowers draw bees, especially large carpenter bees, and butterflies. As a legume it associates with nitrogen-fixing soil bacteria that enrich the ground around it.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium deciduous to semi-evergreen tree with a spreading, open crown.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Long, pendulous chains (racemes) of fragrant five-petaled golden-yellow flowers in late spring and summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Long, cylindrical, dark brown to black seed pods up to about 2 feet, with seeds in sticky pulp.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnately compound with several pairs of large oval leaflets.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: In bloom, the hanging golden flower chains are unmistakable. Out of bloom, look for the long, dark, sausage-like pods dangling from the branches.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive when young

edibility.detail: Not a food plant. The pod pulp is used medicinally as a laxative, not eaten casually.

toxicity.people: Mild toxicity. The pulp and seeds are a strong laxative — eating much causes cramping, nausea, and diarrhea. Not dangerous in passing, but not for snacking.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. The pods and seeds can cause vomiting and diarrhea in dogs (Cassia/Senna group). Keep fallen pods picked up.

quick_hits: Crack the thin tan shell and inside is translucent, juicy flesh around a single dark seed — the reason for the name "dragon's eye."
It is the close cousin of the lychee, with a similar sweet, musky flavor.
The fruit hangs in heavy drooping clusters, sometimes by the hundreds.
That single glossy black seed peeking through pale flesh looks startlingly like an eyeball — hence the Chinese name.
species.origin: Native to southern Asia, from southern China through Southeast Asia. Long cultivated for its fruit and now grown commercially in tropical and subtropical regions, including South and Central Florida.
more_information: Longan is a member of the soapberry family and a close relative of the lychee and rambutan. Its fruit is smaller and less flashy than lychee but keeps and ships better, and many find the flavor sweeter and muskier.

The name "dragon's eye" comes from the peeled fruit: a sphere of translucent, pale flesh with a single round, glossy black seed showing through the center — convincingly eye-like. The flesh is eaten fresh, dried, or in soups and desserts across Asia.
The tree is a heavy, sometimes erratic bearer — loading branches with hanging clusters in good years — and prefers a warm climate with a brief cool spell to trigger flowering.
wildlife_value: The small cream flowers are excellent bee plants and a notable nectar and honey source. Ripe and fallen fruit feeds birds and small mammals.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium evergreen tree with a dense, rounded, spreading canopy.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, yellowish-cream, in large branched clusters; rich in nectar.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, tan to light brown, thin-shelled, in drooping clusters; pale juicy flesh around one shiny black seed.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnately compound, glossy, with leathery oblong leaflets.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Heavy hanging clusters of round, tan-shelled fruit. Peel one to find the translucent flesh and single dark "dragon's eye" seed.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: needs warmth; a brief cool, dry spell helps trigger flowering

edibility.detail: Edible. The translucent flesh is eaten fresh or dried; the single seed is not eaten.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people. A common edible fruit; the hard seed is a choking hazard, not a poison.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The flesh is harmless, but the large round seed is a choking and obstruction hazard — keep fallen fruit picked up.

quick_hits: Ripe, the fruit looks like a green tomato but scoops out like rich chocolate pudding — earning it the name "chocolate pudding fruit."
There is no actual chocolate involved; the flavor and custard texture are entirely the fruit's own.
It must be eaten dead-ripe and soft. Unripe, it is hard, bitter, and astringent.
It is a persimmon relative, and that mahogany-dark, sweet flesh is the family's strangest member.
species.origin: Native to Mexico, Central America, and Colombia. Long cultivated in its homeland and now grown in tropical and subtropical regions, including South and Central Florida.
more_information: Black sapote is a persimmon — a member of the ebony family — with a fruit unlike any other. Green and tomato-like on the outside, it ripens internally to a soft, dark brown, custardy pulp that genuinely resembles chocolate pudding in look and texture, with a mild, sweet flavor.

Timing is everything. Picked or eaten too soon, the fruit is rock-hard, chalky, and mouth-puckeringly astringent. Allowed to soften until the skin browns and the flesh turns almost black and jelly-like, it becomes a dessert in its own right, often blended with a little citrus or folded into baked goods.
The tree is handsome and glossy-leaved, and its dark, dense wood links it to true ebony, the prized timber of the same genus.
wildlife_value: The small white flowers attract bees and other pollinators. Ripe and dropped fruit is taken by birds and small mammals, and the dense evergreen canopy offers cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium to large evergreen tree with a dense, rounded canopy of glossy leaves.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, white to greenish, tucked among the leaves.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, tomato-like, olive-green ripening to brown-black; soft dark pudding-like pulp inside.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, leathery, elliptic, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Round green "tomatoes" hanging among glossy leaves. A ripe one is soft and dull-skinned; cut it to find the dark, custardy flesh.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; prefers warm, frost-free sites

edibility.detail: Edible when fully ripe and soft — sweet, custard-like, eaten fresh or in desserts. Unripe fruit is hard, bitter, and astringent.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people from the ripe fruit. Unripe fruit is unpleasantly astringent but not poisonous.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Ripe flesh is harmless in small amounts; unripe fruit and seeds may upset a dog's stomach.

quick_hits: A true Florida-native tree that grows with its feet in the water — in swamps, pond edges, and even brackish mangrove margins.
Its yellow-green fruit gives it the names pond apple and alligator apple, after the gators that share its swampy haunts and eat the fallen fruit.
The fruit floats, and so do the seeds — the tree spreads by letting moving water carry its crop downstream.
It is a close cousin of the custard apple and soursop, with similar pale, custard-textured flesh.
species.origin: Native to Florida, the Caribbean, tropical Central and South America, and West Africa. In Florida it is a characteristic tree of freshwater swamps, pond and lake margins, sloughs, and the upstream edges of mangrove wetlands.
more_information: Pond apple is one of the few trees genuinely at home in standing water. It rings Florida's swamps, ponds, and sloughs and pushes right up to the brackish edge of mangrove country, tolerating flooding and some salt where most trees would drown. Its trunk often flares into a swollen, buttressed base for stability in soft, wet ground.

The common names tell the story of its place. "Pond apple" marks its habitat; "alligator apple" nods to the gators that share the swamp and eat the dropped fruit. The yellow-green fruit is apple- to melon-sized, with soft, pale, custard-like flesh — a hint of its kinship with custard apple and soursop in the genus Annona.

Water does the dispersing. Both the buoyant fruit and its flattened seeds float, drifting on swamp currents and tides to sprout elsewhere — a neat fit for a tree that lives where the water moves. One caution: the pulp is edible but poor, while the seeds and bark contain toxic compounds and should not be eaten.
wildlife_value: A valuable native wetland tree. Its fruit is eaten by raccoons, birds, turtles, fish, and alligators, and it is the larval host for the giant sphinx moth (Cocytius antaeus), the largest sphinx moth in North America. Growing in flooded ground, it provides cover, perches, and nesting habitat at the water's edge and helps stabilize swampy shorelines.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A small to medium deciduous to semi-evergreen wetland tree (bare-branched in winter), often with a swollen, buttressed base, growing in or beside water.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Solitary, pale yellow to cream, three-petaled, with a reddish inner blotch.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Yellow-green, apple- to melon-sized, with soft pale custard-like flesh; floats.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, leathery, oblong, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A glossy-leaved tree (leafless in winter) standing in or right beside the water, often with a flared base, hung with yellow-green apple-like fruit. Its waterside footing is itself the best clue.

size.habit: upright, often with a buttressed base

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: muck · sand · tolerates prolonged flooding and some brackish water
growing_conditions.note: a wetland specialist for pond margins and swampy ground

edibility.detail: The fruit pulp is edible but bland and poor in quality — eaten more by wildlife than people, occasionally used for jelly. The seeds are toxic and must not be eaten.

toxicity.people: The ripe pulp is edible, but the seeds and bark contain toxic compounds (annonaceous acetogenins) and should not be eaten. Avoid the seeds.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. The seeds and bark contain toxic compounds; keep pets from chewing the fruit and seeds (not specifically listed by ASPCA).

invasive.notes: Native to Florida — not invasive here, and a valued wetland tree. (It has become invasive elsewhere, notably in Australia and Sri Lanka, but it belongs in Florida's swamps.)

quick_hits: The avocado is a berry — botanically a single-seeded berry — despite its size and buttery, savory flesh.
Its flowers play a clever trick: each opens as female one day and male the next, timing the switch to avoid pollinating itself.
The creamy fruit is rich in healthy fats, which is unusual — most fruits store energy as sugar, not oil.
Delicious to people, but the leaves, skin, and pit contain persin, which is toxic to dogs, cats, and especially birds.
species.origin: Native to south-central Mexico and Central America, where it has been cultivated for thousands of years. Now grown worldwide in warm climates, with Florida (especially the south) a major U.S. growing region.
more_information: The avocado has been part of Mesoamerican life for millennia, long enough that its evolutionary partners — the giant ground sloths and other megafauna that once swallowed the fruit whole and dispersed its huge seed — have been extinct for thousands of years. The tree carries on, now spread by people instead.

Its flowers are a marvel of anti-inbreeding design. Each flower opens twice: functioning as female on the first opening and male on the second, with the timing staggered so a tree tends to shed pollen when its own flowers are receptive as female, encouraging cross-pollination between trees.

Unusually for a fruit, the avocado stores its energy as oil rather than sugar, which gives the flesh its rich, buttery quality. One caution worth posting: the leaves, bark, skin, and pit contain a compound called persin that is harmless to people in the fruit but toxic to many animals, birds most of all.
wildlife_value: The small flowers are visited by bees and other insects. Birds and mammals eat fallen fruit, and the large evergreen canopy provides shade, cover, and nesting sites.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium to large evergreen tree with a broad, often spreading or dense canopy.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, greenish-yellow, in large branched clusters; each flower opens as female then male.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: A large, pear-shaped to round berry with leathery green to black skin, buttery flesh, and one very large seed.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, glossy, elliptic, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A leafy evergreen tree hung with pear-shaped fruit and a single oversized pit inside. Cut fruit shows the smooth pale-green to yellow flesh.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · slightly alkaline to acidic · must be well-drained
growing_conditions.note: hates wet feet — root rot is the main hazard in Florida; frost-sensitive

edibility.detail: Edible. The buttery flesh is widely eaten; the skin and pit are not eaten.

toxicity.people: No toxicity to people from the fruit. (The leaves and pit contain persin, but people eat only the flesh.)
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. Avocado leaves, skin, and pit contain persin, which can cause vomiting and diarrhea in dogs, and the large pit is a serious choking and obstruction hazard. Avocado is far more dangerous to birds (ASPCA).

quick_hits: A pineapple is not one fruit but many — dozens of separate flowers fused together into a single juicy mass.
It is a bromeliad, a cousin of Spanish moss and the air plants that perch in Florida's oak trees.
It takes its time: a plant needs about two years to produce one fruit.
The enzyme bromelain in fresh pineapple actually digests protein — which is why fresh pineapple can make your tongue tingle.
species.origin: Native to South America, likely the Paraná-Paraguay River region of Brazil and Paraguay. Carried across the tropics by early peoples and now grown worldwide; it thrives in warm, frost-free Florida.
more_information: The pineapple is a bromeliad, part of the same family as the Spanish moss and air plants that drape Florida's trees — but unlike its perching relatives, it grows from the ground in a rosette of stiff, spiny leaves.

The "fruit" is really a community. Each pineapple forms when dozens of individual flowers on a spike swell and fuse together around the central stalk, so what looks like one fruit is many fused into a so-called multiple fruit, capped with a leafy crown that can be planted to grow a new plant.

Patience is required: from planting, a pineapple typically takes around two years to flower and fruit. Fresh fruit also contains bromelain, an enzyme that breaks down protein — harmless but the reason fresh (not canned) pineapple can sting the tongue and is used as a meat tenderizer.

There is a Florida twist hiding in the family tree. Pineapple's wild cousins — the native airplants and Spanish moss draped through Florida's oaks — are under siege from the "evil weevil" (the Mexican bromeliad weevil), an invasive beetle that arrived on imported bromeliads in 1989 and has killed off native bromeliads across the state; several Florida airplants are now listed as endangered. The weevil will even bore into a pineapple.
wildlife_value: The tubular flowers can attract hummingbirds and bees in bloom. The spiny rosette offers some ground-level cover, and ripe fruit draws insects and small animals.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A low, ground-dwelling rosette of stiff, sword-shaped leaves, often spiny-edged.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Many small purple-blue flowers on a central spike, fusing into the fruit.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: The familiar multiple fruit — fused, juicy, golden inside — topped with a leafy crown.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Long, narrow, rigid, gray-green, in a spreading rosette.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A spiky ground rosette with a single fruit rising from the center on a short stalk, crowned with a tuft of leaves.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · well-drained; tolerates poor soil
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; the leafy crown can be planted to grow a new plant

edibility.detail: Edible. The juicy golden fruit is eaten fresh, cooked, or juiced.

toxicity.people: No toxicity to people. Fresh fruit contains bromelain, which can irritate the mouth in excess but is not dangerous.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs (ASPCA lists pineapple as non-toxic). The tough core and spiny leaves can cause stomach upset or injury if chewed.

quick_hits: Jackfruit produces the largest fruit of any tree on Earth — a single one can weigh over 100 pounds.
Those giant fruits grow straight from the trunk and main branches, not out on the twigs.
Ripe, it smells and tastes sweetly tropical; unripe and cooked, it shreds into a famous meat substitute.
The whole tree runs with sticky white latex — handling the fruit without oiled hands is a gluey mistake.
species.origin: Native to the rainforests of the Western Ghats of India and surrounding South and Southeast Asia. Cultivated across the tropics for thousands of years and grown in frost-free South and Central Florida.
more_information: The jackfruit is a record-holder: the largest tree-borne fruit in the world, with individual fruits regularly topping fifty pounds and sometimes exceeding a hundred. To carry that weight, the tree does something unusual — it produces fruit directly on the trunk and major limbs (a habit called cauliflory) rather than on slender outer branches.

Inside the knobby green rind are dozens of sweet, golden, edible bulbs surrounding large seeds, all embedded in stringy fibers. Ripe jackfruit is fragrant and sweet; young, unripe jackfruit is starchy and neutral, and when cooked it pulls apart into tender shreds that have made it a popular plant-based stand-in for pulled meat.
Every part of the tree exudes a sticky white latex, so harvesting and cutting jackfruit is traditionally done with oiled hands and knives to keep the glue at bay.
wildlife_value: Fallen and overripe fruit is a magnet for insects, birds, and mammals. The large evergreen canopy provides dense shade, cover, and nesting habitat.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A large evergreen tree with a dense, rounded crown.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny, in fleshy spikes borne on the trunk and branches; male and female on the same tree.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Enormous, oblong, green to yellow-brown, knobby-skinned, growing from the trunk and limbs; sweet golden bulbs and large seeds inside.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, glossy, leathery, dark green, oval.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Huge knobby fruit attached directly to the trunk and big branches — a sight no other park tree offers. Glossy dark leaves and milky sap confirm it.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · acidic to slightly alkaline · deep, well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; needs room — the fruit is heavy and the tree is large

edibility.detail: Edible. Ripe bulbs are eaten fresh; unripe fruit is cooked as a vegetable and meat substitute; the seeds are eaten boiled or roasted.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people. The sticky latex is messy and can irritate skin but is not poisonous.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The flesh is harmless in small amounts; large seeds are a choking hazard and the latex can upset a dog's stomach.

quick_hits: The drooping yellow flowers are the source of ylang-ylang oil — one of the most important ingredients in perfume, including the original Chanel No. 5.
The scent is richest at night, which is when the tree releases its heaviest fragrance.
The name comes from a Tagalog word often translated as "flower of flowers."
The curly, ribbon-like petals look more like dangling sea creatures than typical blossoms.
species.origin: Native to the Philippines, Indonesia, and surrounding tropical Asia and the western Pacific. Cultivated across the tropics for its perfume flowers and grown in frost-free Florida.
more_information: Ylang-ylang is grown the world over for one reason: its fragrance. The fresh flowers are steam-distilled into an essential oil prized in perfumery for a rich, sweet, slightly fruity scent, and it remains a backbone note in countless perfumes — most famously the original Chanel No. 5.

The tree concentrates its fragrance for the night, when the smell becomes most intense, a strategy aimed at night-flying moth pollinators. Its flowers are as odd as they are aromatic: long, narrow, curling yellow-green petals that hang in ribbony clusters and look almost like drooping starfish.
The common name comes to us from Tagalog and is often translated as "flower of flowers," a fitting tag for a tree grown purely for its perfume.
wildlife_value: The fragrant night-scented flowers attract moths and other nocturnal pollinators. The tree's fruit clusters feed birds, and the canopy offers cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A fast-growing evergreen tree with somewhat drooping, brittle branches.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Drooping clusters of long, narrow, curling petals, green aging to deep yellow; intensely fragrant, especially at night.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Clusters of small green berries ripening to near-black.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, oval, pointed, with wavy margins.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Hanging clusters of curly, ribbon-like yellow flowers — and the scent, which is the real signature. Crush nothing; just stand near a flowering tree at dusk.

size.height: 30 to 50 feet (often kept lower in cultivation)

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained, rich
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; branches are brittle and can break in wind

edibility.detail: Not a food plant. Grown for its perfume flowers, the source of ylang-ylang essential oil.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity from the growing plant. The concentrated essential oil can irritate or sensitize skin in some people.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The plant is not known to be harmful, though concentrated oils should be kept away from pets.

quick_hits: The popular houseplant with a braided trunk is this tree — the braiding is done by hand on young, flexible saplings.
In the wild it is no tabletop plant: it grows into a substantial tree with showy, brush-like flowers.
It is wrapped in good-luck lore, said in feng shui to attract prosperity — hence "money tree."
Its large nuts are edible roasted, tasting something like chestnuts or peanuts.
species.origin: Native to wetlands and riverbanks of Central and South America, from Mexico to northern South America. Widely grown as an ornamental and houseplant, and outdoors in frost-free Florida.
more_information: Most people meet the money tree as a small braided houseplant, and the braid is no accident of nature — growers weave the supple young trunks together by hand, and the plant grows on with the braid locked in. Left to itself outdoors, it becomes a real tree with a swollen-based trunk suited to its wet native haunts.

In flower it is striking: large buds split to release long, brush-like sprays of cream stamens, followed by big woody seed pods. Inside those pods are the nuts, which are edible roasted and reportedly taste of chestnut or peanut — a genuine if little-used food.
The "money" name comes from feng shui tradition, where the plant is associated with prosperity and good fortune, which is much of why it became such a ubiquitous gift plant.
wildlife_value: The large nectar-rich, brush-like flowers attract bats, moths, and other pollinators in the tropics. The seeds and pods feed wildlife where it grows naturally.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium evergreen tree with a green, often swollen-based trunk; shrubby and braided in cultivation.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Large buds opening to long, showy brushes of cream-white stamens.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Large, woody, oval seed pods containing several rounded nuts.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Palmately compound, with five to nine glossy leaflets spreading like fingers from the leaf stalk.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Hand-shaped (palmate) leaves of five or more leaflets and, in cultivation, the distinctive braided trunk. Flowering specimens show big brush-like cream blooms.

size.height: 30 to 60 feet in the ground (much smaller potted)

growing_conditions.light: full sun to bright shade (tolerates indoor light)
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · rich, moist; tolerates wet ground
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; happiest with steady moisture

edibility.detail: The roasted nuts are edible and reportedly chestnut- or peanut-like. Not a common food plant here.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs — Pachira aquatica (money tree) is listed as non-toxic by ASPCA.

quick_hits: This is the Florida state tree — the cabbage palm, found in every county in the state.
The growing bud at its center is the original "swamp cabbage," or heart of palm, a regional delicacy.
Harvesting that heart, though, kills the palm, since it is the single growing point the whole tree depends on.
Its criss-crossed leaf bases ("boots") create perches and pockets that ferns, vines, and small animals colonize.
species.origin: Native to the southeastern United States, throughout Florida and along the coastal plain. It grows in an exceptionally wide range of habitats — hammocks, marsh edges, dunes, and pine flatwoods — and tolerates salt, flooding, drought, and fire.
more_information: The cabbage palm is Florida's state tree and arguably its most characteristic, growing from the Keys to the Panhandle and shrugging off salt spray, flooding, drought, hurricanes, and fire. That toughness comes from a single, protected growing point (the "heart" or terminal bud) buried at the top of the trunk.

That heart is also the catch. Cut from the tree, the tender bud is the famous "swamp cabbage" or heart of palm — a Cracker Florida tradition still celebrated at festivals. But because a palm has only that one growing point, harvesting the heart kills the whole tree, which is why it is a treat to be taken thoughtfully and never from wild stands without care.

Older trunks may keep their crisscrossing old leaf bases, called boots, or shed them to a smooth gray column. Either way the palm is a habitat in itself — boots and leaf crowns host ferns, vines, insects, and nesting birds.
Two more notes worth knowing: it is one of the most hurricane-resistant trees in Florida, and it is the official state tree of two states — Florida and South Carolina, the "Palmetto State."
wildlife_value: The big sprays of white flowers are a major nectar source for bees (the basis of prized palm honey) and many insects. The black fruits feed raccoons, birds, and other wildlife, and the leaf-base "boots" and crown shelter ferns, insects, bats, and nesting birds.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A single-trunked fan palm with a rounded crown of large, arching leaves.

blocks.label: Trunk
blocks.text: Stout, gray, either smooth or covered in crisscrossing old leaf bases ("boots").

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Long, branching sprays of small, fragrant, creamy-white flowers in summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, round, shiny black drupes in large hanging clusters.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large costapalmate (fan-like with an arching midrib), giving the leaf a folded, curved look.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A classic fan palm with a single gray trunk and curved, fan-shaped leaves. The arching midrib that runs partway into the fan separates it from true fan palms.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · clay · loam · acidic to alkaline · tolerates flooding and drought
growing_conditions.note: extremely tough — handles salt, flood, drought, and fire

edibility.detail: The growing bud (swamp cabbage / heart of palm) is edible and a Florida tradition — but harvesting it kills the palm. The small fruits are also edible but bland.

toxicity.people: No toxicity to people.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Cabbage palm is not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida — the state tree. Not invasive.

quick_hits: In summer it bursts into clouds of crinkled, crepe-paper flowers — the source of both its beauty and its name.
Mature trees shed their bark in patches, leaving a smooth, mottled, almost muscular trunk.
The bloom runs for weeks, and trimming spent flowers can coax a second flush.
It is one of the most planted flowering trees in the South — for good reason.
species.origin: Native to East and Southeast Asia, especially China and Korea. Introduced to the southern United States in the 1700s and now one of the most widely planted ornamental flowering trees across the warm South, including Florida.
more_information: Crape-myrtles earn their place in southern gardens with a long, lavish summer bloom. The flowers have a distinctive crinkled, crepe-paper texture — the source of the name — and come in white, pink, red, lavender, and purple, often carried for many weeks.

The show does not end with the flowers. As the trees mature, the outer bark flakes away in patches to reveal a smooth, sinuous, mottled trunk in shades of tan, gray, and cinnamon — a feature as prized in winter as the flowers are in summer.
The park's specimen is identified at the genus level (Lagerstroemia); the many garden crape-myrtles are mostly hybrids, and a firm species name will be added once it can be confirmed.

A local note: please resist "crape murder" — the harsh topping-back to knuckled stubs seen across so many Florida yards is unnecessary, and it spoils both the tree's natural vase shape and the beautiful peeling bark.
wildlife_value: The pollen-rich flowers are heavily worked by bees and other pollinators through the summer. Birds eat the small seed capsules in fall and winter, and the branching canopy offers perches and cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A deciduous large shrub or small multi-trunked tree with an open, vase-shaped crown.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Crinkled, crepe-paper-textured blooms in dense clusters at branch tips, summer into fall; white to pink, red, or purple.

blocks.label: Bark
blocks.text: Flakes in patches on older wood, leaving a smooth, mottled tan-and-gray trunk.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Small, oval, glossy, often coloring orange-red before dropping.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Crinkled summer flower clusters and a smooth, peeling, mottled trunk. The crepe-paper petal texture is the clincher.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: powdery mildew can affect older varieties in humidity; good air flow helps

edibility.detail: Not edible. Grown as an ornamental.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people. Considered non-toxic.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs — crape-myrtle is listed as non-toxic by ASPCA.

quick_hits: Despite the name, it is neither a pine nor a palm — its leaves spiral up the stem like the thread of a screw.
Many species stand on a cage of stilt-like prop roots that lift the trunk clear off the ground.
Those aerial roots brace the plant against wind and shifting, often coastal, soils.
Some screwpines bear big, knobby fruit that looks startlingly like a green pineapple or a brain.
species.origin: Native to the Old World tropics — coastal and island habitats across the Pacific, Southeast Asia, Africa, and Australia. Various species are grown ornamentally in frost-free regions including Florida.
more_information: Screwpines (Pandanus) are unmistakable once you know them: long, strap-shaped, often spiny-edged leaves arranged in a tight spiral that climbs the stem like screw threads — the source of the common name, even though the plant is unrelated to pines.

Many species are propped up on a tepee of stout aerial "stilt roots" that grow down from the trunk into the soil, bracing the plant against wind and erosion in the exposed coastal and island habitats where it often lives. The effect is of a plant standing on its own scaffolding.

The genus is also useful: some species produce large, segmented fruits (resembling a knobby pineapple) that are eaten in the Pacific, and the fragrant leaves of one relative, pandan, are a beloved Southeast Asian flavoring. The park's plant is identified to genus; a species name will follow once confirmed.
wildlife_value: The fruit of fruiting species feeds birds, bats, and other animals, and the dense, spiny rosettes provide protected nesting and shelter. The prop-root tangles offer cover for small creatures.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A palm-like evergreen shrub or tree, often branched, frequently raised on stilt roots.

blocks.label: Roots
blocks.text: Stout aerial prop roots descending from the trunk to the ground in many species.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Long, strap-shaped, leathery, spiraling up the stem, usually with spiny margins and midribs.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: In fruiting species, a large, round to oval, segmented head resembling a knobby pineapple.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Spiraling strap-leaves and, often, a basket of stilt roots holding the trunk off the ground. The spiral arrangement is the signature.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · tolerant of poor and salty coastal soils
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; leaf margins can be sharply spiny

edibility.detail: Varies by species. Some screwpines have edible fruit (Pacific species) or fragrant flavoring leaves (pandan); others are ornamental only.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity reported. The spiny leaf margins are the main hazard.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be harmful, though the spiny leaves can cause injury.

quick_hits: The dazzling color is not the flower — it comes from papery modified leaves called bracts, surrounding tiny true flowers.
The actual flowers are small, white, and tucked in threes inside each splash of color.
It blooms hardest when stressed: lean, dry conditions trigger the heaviest color.
Watch the stems — they are armed with sharp, hooked thorns.
species.origin: Native to South America, especially Brazil, Peru, and Argentina. Now one of the most widely planted tropical ornamental vines worldwide, thriving in warm, sunny Florida.
more_information: Bougainvillea's famous color is a trick of the leaves. The vivid magenta, orange, purple, red, or white "petals" are actually bracts — modified leaves — arranged in threes, each cradling a small, tubular, cream-white true flower at its center. The bracts last far longer than ordinary petals, which is why a plant can blaze with color for months.

It is also a plant that rewards a little hardship. Bougainvillea blooms most heavily in full sun and lean, somewhat dry conditions; pampered with rich soil and too much water, it tends to grow lush and green with fewer bracts. That preference suits Florida's sunny, sandy sites well.

The trade-off is the thorns. Vigorous, scrambling, and armed with stout hooked spines, bougainvillea climbs and sprawls readily and needs space and respect when pruned. The park's plant is identified at the genus level.

There is a remarkable backstory to the name. Bougainvillea was collected on the first French voyage around the world (1766–1769) and named for its commander, Louis-Antoine de Bougainville — but the person who most likely gathered it was Jeanne Baret, the expedition's botanical assistant. She had joined disguised as a man, because women were barred from French naval ships, and in doing so became the first woman known to circumnavigate the globe.
wildlife_value: The bracts and small flowers attract butterflies, bees, and hummingbirds. The dense, thorny tangle provides excellent protected nesting and shelter cover for small birds.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A vigorous, scrambling, thorny evergreen vine or mounding shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny, white, tubular true flowers in threes, each surrounded by showy papery bracts in magenta, purple, red, orange, or white.

blocks.label: Stems
blocks.text: Woody, arching, armed with sharp hooked thorns.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Oval, pointed, medium green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Masses of papery color on a thorny, climbing plant. Look closely for the small white flowers inside each set of three bracts.

size.height: 15 to 30 feet as a climber (kept smaller as a shrub)

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · well-drained; tolerates poor soil
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: high — and lean, dry conditions boost bloom
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; sharp thorns — site away from paths

edibility.detail: Not edible. Grown as an ornamental.

toxicity.people: Low toxicity. The sap can cause mild skin irritation, and the thorns can cause puncture wounds, but it is not poisonous to eat.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The main hazard is mechanical — the sharp thorns, not poison.

quick_hits: The dense, rounded clusters of small tubular flowers give it the nickname "flame of the woods."
Each bloom is a slender tube — perfectly shaped for the butterflies that probe them for nectar.
It flowers in flushes much of the year in Florida's warm climate.
In parts of Asia the flowers carry religious and ceremonial meaning.
species.origin: Native to tropical Asia, with the most common ornamental forms tracing to India and Sri Lanka. Now a staple flowering shrub across the tropics and a mainstay of warm-climate Florida landscapes.
more_information: Ixora is grown for its flowers — tight, rounded clusters of small, four-petaled tubular blooms in fiery red, orange, pink, or yellow, set against glossy evergreen leaves. The massed effect gives it common names like "flame of the woods," and in Florida's warmth it blooms in repeated flushes through much of the year.
The long, narrow flower tubes are a clue to its pollinators: butterflies and other long-tongued insects can reach the nectar at the base, making a healthy ixora hedge a reliable butterfly stop.

The plant carries cultural weight in its homeland as well, where the flowers feature in Hindu worship and ceremony. The park's plant is identified at the genus level; the species or hybrid will be added once confirmed.
wildlife_value: The nectar-rich tubular flowers are a steady draw for butterflies and other pollinators. The dense evergreen shrub provides good cover and some nesting habitat for small birds.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A dense, rounded evergreen shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tight, rounded clusters of small, four-petaled, long-tubed flowers in red, orange, pink, or yellow; blooming in flushes much of the year.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, leathery, opposite, oblong, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Domed clusters of small tubular flowers on a tidy, glossy-leaved shrub. The dense "bouquet" flower heads are characteristic.

growing_conditions.light: full sun to part sun (best bloom in sun)
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · acidic preferred · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; prefers acidic soil — leaves yellow in alkaline conditions

edibility.detail: Not a food plant. Grown as an ornamental.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people reported.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be harmful.

alternate_names: Star Jasmine (a name shared with other plants)
quick_hits: The pure white pinwheel flowers open from neat reddish-purple buds, a two-tone effect that looks like the plant is decorating itself.
The scent is sweetest in the evening, when the blooms perfume the air around them.
It is a true jasmine (Jasminum) — not one of the many unrelated plants that borrow the name.
A scrambling, semi-vining shrub, it can be grown as a mound, a hedge, or a loose climber.
species.origin: Native to South and Southeast Asia. Widely cultivated across the tropics and subtropics as a fragrant ornamental, and grown in warm, frost-free Florida.
more_information: Angel-wing jasmine is grown for its fragrant, pure-white, pinwheel-shaped flowers, which open in clusters from striking reddish-purple buds — so a plant in bloom shows both the dark buds and the white open flowers at once, a pleasing two-tone effect.

Like other true jasmines, it saves its strongest perfume for the evening and night, releasing sweet fragrance to attract its moth pollinators. It is a genuine Jasminum, worth distinguishing from the many unrelated "jasmines" (Confederate jasmine, Carolina jessamine, night-blooming jasmine) that share the name but not the genus.
Its loose, semi-climbing habit makes it flexible in the landscape — left alone it mounds and scrambles, and with support it will clamber as a light vine.
wildlife_value: The fragrant, night-scented flowers attract moths, butterflies, and other pollinators. The dense, scrambling growth provides cover and nesting sites for small birds.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A scrambling, semi-vining evergreen shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: White, pinwheel-shaped, fragrant, opening from reddish-purple buds in clusters; most fragrant in the evening.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, dark green, oval, in opposite or whorled arrangement.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: White pinwheel flowers paired with dark purple-red buds on a glossy, loosely climbing shrub. The evening fragrance confirms a true jasmine.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; can be grown as a hedge, mound, or light climber

edibility.detail: Not a food plant. Grown as a fragrant ornamental.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people. True jasmines (Jasminum) are generally regarded as non-toxic.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs — true jasmine (Jasminum) is listed as non-toxic by ASPCA.

quick_hits: Scratch a leaf and the mark stays for the life of that leaf — which is how the tree earned its name.
The leaves are so thick and leathery that people once used them as playing cards and writing paper.
It often starts life as an epiphyte, sprouting in another tree's crook before sending roots down to the ground.
The sticky sap was historically used as a crude glue and even to caulk boats — but it is an irritant, so look, do not carve.
species.origin: Native to the Caribbean, southern Florida, and tropical Central and South America. Grown as an ornamental in frost-free regions and naturalized in parts of South Florida.
more_information: The autograph tree's claim to fame is its leaves: thick, stiff, and leathery enough that a fingernail or stick leaves a permanent etched scar, which darkens and persists as long as the leaf lives. People have signed them, used them as note paper, and even dealt them as makeshift playing cards.

It has an unusual way of getting started, too. Seeds often sprout high in the crotch of another tree as an epiphyte, then send long roots down to the ground — a strangler-fig-like strategy that lets it begin life above the competition.

A word of caution behind the novelty: the plant's milky-yellow sap (latex) is sticky and irritating, historically pressed into service as a glue and boat caulk. It is best admired, not carved — and the showy white-and-pink flowers and round green fruit are reason enough to leave the leaves alone.
wildlife_value: The flowers attract bees and other pollinators. The fruit splits to reveal seeds in red pulp that birds eat and disperse, and the dense evergreen canopy offers cover and nesting sites.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: An evergreen shrub or small tree, sometimes starting as an epiphyte with dangling roots.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Showy, white with a pink or yellow center, somewhat lotus-like.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, green, leathery; splits open to show seeds in bright red pulp.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Very thick, stiff, leathery, paddle-shaped, dark glossy green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Thick, leathery paddle leaves — the obvious test is a light scratch, which leaves a lasting mark. Round green fruit and lotus-like white flowers confirm it.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · limestone · salt-tolerant · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; tough and adaptable in warm coastal sites

edibility.detail: Not edible. The fruit and sap are not eaten.

toxicity.people: Mildly toxic. The sticky latex sap is irritating to skin and eyes, and the fruit and seeds are not for eating.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. The irritant latex sap and the fruit can cause mouth irritation and stomach upset in dogs.

invasive.notes: Native to extreme South Florida but can spread from cultivation; not on Florida's main invasive lists, though plant thoughtfully in warm regions.

quick_hits: It looks like a stiff, low palm or fern, but it is a cycad — an ancient seed plant from the age of the dinosaurs.
The thick, leathery leaflets feel almost like cardboard, which is where the name comes from.
Every part is highly poisonous to people and pets — the seeds especially can be deadly to dogs.
Like all cycads, it partners with bacteria in special roots to pull nitrogen from the air, letting it thrive in poor soil.
species.origin: Native to a small area of eastern Mexico (Veracruz). Despite its limited wild range, it is one of the most widely planted ornamental cycads in warm climates, including Florida.
more_information: Cycads are living fossils — a lineage of seed plants that flourished alongside the dinosaurs and have changed little since. The cardboard cycad is one of the most popular in cultivation, prized for its low, symmetrical rosette of stiff, dark green, pinnate leaves whose thick, blunt leaflets have a distinctly papery, cardboard-like feel.

Beneath the handsome looks is a serious hazard. Every part of the plant contains cycasin and related toxins, and it is dangerously poisonous to people, dogs, cats, and livestock. The seeds are the most toxic part and are a frequent cause of severe, often fatal poisoning in dogs that chew them — a genuine concern wherever pets and cardboard cycads share a space.

Like other cycads, it forms specialized upward-growing "coralloid" roots that house nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria, a partnership that lets it thrive in poor, sandy soils. It also bears its seeds in cones rather than flowers, on separate male and female plants.

It also has a Florida cousin worth knowing. The cardboard cycad's genus, Zamia, includes the only cycad native to the United States — the coontie (Zamia integrifolia), a Florida native and the sole host plant of the atala butterfly. When coontie was nearly wiped out by over-harvesting for its starch ("Florida arrowroot"), the atala was thought extinct — until coontie was replanted across South Florida and the butterfly came roaring back, one of the state's great conservation comebacks.
wildlife_value: Wildlife value is limited by the plant's toxicity. In its native range, specialized cycad-feeding insects and beetles pollinate the cones; the toxic seeds are not a safe food source for local wildlife and pets.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A low, stiff, palm-like cycad with a short, often partly buried trunk and a symmetrical rosette of leaves.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnate, dark green, with thick, leathery, blunt, cardboard-textured leaflets; new leaves emerge in a flush.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: By cones, not flowers; male and female cones on separate plants.

blocks.label: Seeds
blocks.text: Borne in the female cone; large and highly toxic.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A short, stiff rosette of fern- or palm-like leaves with notably thick, firm, blunt leaflets. The cardboard feel of the leaflets and the cone (not flower) is the tell.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · well-drained; tolerates poor soil
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive in hard freezes; very low-maintenance once established

edibility.detail: Not edible — highly toxic. No part of the plant should ever be eaten.

toxicity.people: Highly toxic if ingested. Contains cycasin and related toxins that can cause severe vomiting, liver damage, and neurological effects. The seeds are the most dangerous part. Seek immediate medical care if any part is eaten.
toxicity.dogs: Highly toxic to dogs — cycads (Zamia and relatives) are listed as toxic by ASPCA and are a leading cause of fatal plant poisoning in dogs. The seeds are especially deadly, causing liver failure. Keep pets well away and pick up any dropped seeds.

quick_hits: A big, soft-looking shrub with broad gray-green leaves and crown-shaped purple-and-white flowers — and milky, poisonous sap throughout.
It is a powerhouse butterfly plant, serving as a host for monarchs and queens in warm regions.
The sap carries potent toxins (cardiac glycosides) that protect both the plant and the caterpillars that eat it.
In parts of Asia the flowers are used in garlands and religious offerings, despite the plant's poison.
species.origin: Native to North and tropical Africa and much of South Asia. Widely naturalized across the warm Americas and grown as a butterfly and ornamental plant in frost-free Florida.
more_information: Giant milkweed (Calotropis) is a fast, soft-wooded shrub with thick, gray-green, fuzzy leaves and clusters of intricate, crown-shaped flowers in white and purple. Like its smaller milkweed relatives, it runs with milky white sap and serves as a host plant for monarch and queen butterfly caterpillars, making it a heavy hitter for a butterfly garden.

That partnership is built on poison. The sap is loaded with cardiac glycosides — the same class of heart-affecting toxins found in other milkweeds — which the caterpillars store to make themselves distasteful to predators. The same compounds make every part of the plant toxic to people and animals if eaten, and the sap can seriously irritate skin and eyes.

Despite its toxicity, the plant has a long human history: its flowers are used in garlands and temple offerings across parts of South Asia, and its fibers and latex have had traditional uses — a reminder that "poisonous" and "valued" often go together.

One honest note for butterfly gardeners: native milkweeds are the best first choice for monarchs. Giant milkweed is a non-native that monarchs will also use, valued partly because it is big enough that caterpillars rarely strip it bare.
wildlife_value: A premier butterfly host plant — monarch and queen caterpillars feed on the leaves and store its toxins for protection. The nectar-rich flowers draw butterflies, bees, and wasps, making it one of the garden's busiest pollinator plants despite its toxicity.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A fast-growing, soft-wooded large shrub or small tree.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Clusters of waxy, star- or crown-shaped flowers in white and purple, much of the year.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Inflated, gray-green, balloon-like pods that split to release silk-tufted seeds.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, broad, gray-green, soft and slightly fuzzy, with milky sap.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Broad gray-green leaves, crown-shaped purple-and-white flowers, and milky sap when a leaf is broken. Inflated pods and feeding monarch or queen caterpillars confirm it.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · poor and dry soils · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; thrives in heat and poor soil; milky sap is an irritant

edibility.detail: Not edible — toxic. No part of the plant should be eaten.

toxicity.people: Toxic if ingested, and the milky sap irritates skin and eyes (it can damage the eye). Contains cardiac glycosides that affect the heart. Keep sap away from eyes and do not eat any part.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs. The cardiac glycosides in the leaves and sap can cause vomiting, heart rhythm problems, and worse. Keep pets from chewing it.

invasive.notes: Naturalized in parts of the warm Americas and can spread; not among Florida's worst invaders but plant thoughtfully and watch for seedlings.

quick_hits: One plant, three flower colors at once: blooms open deep purple, fade to lavender, then white over three days — yesterday, today, and tomorrow.
The shifting colors mean every flowering bush looks tie-dyed in purple and white.
The flowers are sweetly fragrant, especially in the evening.
It is genuinely poisonous — the berries are dangerous, and it is a well-documented cause of seizures in dogs.
species.origin: Native to tropical South America, especially Brazil. Widely grown as a fragrant flowering ornamental in warm climates, including Florida.
more_information: The charm of Brunfelsia is in the timing. Each flower opens a rich purple, fades to pale lavender the next day, and turns nearly white the day after — so a single bush in full bloom carries all three colors at once, the basis for the playful name "yesterday, today, and tomorrow." The flowers are sweetly, sometimes intensely fragrant, especially toward evening.

Behind the pretty face is real toxicity. The plant contains brunfelsamidine and related compounds, and all parts — leaves, flowers, and especially the berry-like fruit — are poisonous. It is a particularly well-documented hazard for dogs: the fruits seem to attract them, and ingestion can cause tremors and seizures, sometimes severe.
It is a relative of nightshades and petunias, a family famous for mixing beauty, food, and poison in roughly equal measure.
wildlife_value: The fragrant flowers attract butterflies, moths, and other pollinators. Wildlife value is otherwise limited by the plant's toxicity, and the berries are not a safe food source for pets.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, berry-like, toxic.

blocks.label: Leaves

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A shrub carrying purple, lavender, and white flowers all at the same time — the color shift is unmistakable. Note any berries and keep pets away from them.

toxicity.people: Toxic if ingested. Contains brunfelsamidine and related toxins that can cause tremors, seizures, and other serious effects. All parts are poisonous; do not eat any part.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs and well documented as a cause of seizures. The berries seem to attract dogs and are especially dangerous; ingestion can cause tremors and convulsions (ASPCA lists Brunfelsia as toxic). Keep pets away.

quick_hits: The big, trumpet-shaped blooms on a tall hollow stalk are the classic "amaryllis" sold around the holidays — though botanically it is Hippeastrum.
Each flower can be six inches or more across, in vivid red, pink, white, or striped.
The whole plant grows from a large bulb, and that bulb is the most poisonous part.
It contains lycorine, the same toxin found in daffodils and crinum lilies.
species.origin: Native to tropical and subtropical South America. Bred extensively into the large-flowered hybrids sold worldwide, and grown as a garden bulb in warm climates including Florida.
more_information: The plants sold as "amaryllis," especially the showy holiday bulbs, are mostly Hippeastrum — a separate genus from the true amaryllis, though the common name has stuck for generations. From a single large bulb rises a tall, hollow stalk topped with two to several enormous trumpet-shaped flowers in red, pink, white, salmon, or candy-striped patterns.

The drama comes with a caution. Like its relatives in the amaryllis family — daffodils, crinums, spider lilies — it contains lycorine and related alkaloids, concentrated most heavily in the bulb. Eating it causes vomiting, drooling, and abdominal upset, and a large dose is more serious.
In Florida's mild climate the bulbs can stay in the ground year-round and multiply, returning to bloom each year rather than being treated as one-time holiday gifts.
wildlife_value: The large flowers can attract bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds when in bloom. Wildlife value is otherwise limited, and the toxic bulbs are not a safe food source.

blocks.label: Leaves

toxicity.people: Toxic if ingested. Contains lycorine and related alkaloids (most concentrated in the bulb) that cause vomiting, drooling, and abdominal pain. Seek care if a significant amount is eaten.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs — amaryllis (Hippeastrum) is listed as toxic by ASPCA. The bulb is the most dangerous part; ingestion causes vomiting, drooling, and lethargy.

quick_hits: It carries sky-blue or purple flowers and clusters of bright golden-orange berries at the same time — a striking two-color show.
Those cheerful golden berries are poisonous, which is the catch worth knowing.
The flowers are excellent butterfly and hummingbird plants, blooming much of the year.
The arching, sometimes thorny stems give the shrub a graceful, weeping look.
species.origin: Native to the Caribbean, Mexico, and Central and South America. Widely grown as a flowering ornamental and hedge across the tropics and warm United States, including Florida.
more_information: Skyflower (Duranta erecta) is a favorite for its long bloom and lively color contrast. Loose sprays of small, five-lobed flowers in sky blue, lavender, or purple appear nearly year-round in Florida's warmth, and because flowering and fruiting overlap, a plant often shows flowers and shining golden-orange berries at once.

Those berries are the warning. Despite looking like edible fruit, the berries and leaves are toxic, containing saponins and other compounds; serious poisonings, including in children, have been recorded, and the plant is dangerous to dogs and livestock as well. It is a plant to enjoy with eyes, not mouths.

For wildlife it is a strong performer — the nectar-rich flowers are reliable butterfly and hummingbird draws — and its arching, sometimes lightly thorny stems give it a graceful, fountain-like shape that suits hedges and specimen plantings alike.
wildlife_value: The nectar-rich flowers are a strong, year-round draw for butterflies and hummingbirds, making it one of the garden's better pollinator shrubs. Some birds eat the berries that are toxic to mammals, but the plant's main value is as a nectar source.

toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs. The berries and foliage can cause vomiting, drowsiness, and more serious effects in dogs and other animals. Keep pets away from the fallen berries.

quick_hits: The crisp white iris-like flowers earn it the name fortnight lily — they tend to bloom in rhythmic flushes, roughly every two weeks.
Each individual bloom lasts only a day or two, but the plant keeps producing them over a long season.
Despite the name, it is not a true lily — it is a member of the iris family.
It grows from tough, grassy, fan-shaped clumps that shrug off heat, drought, and neglect.
species.origin: Native to southern and eastern Africa. Widely planted as a tough, low-care ornamental in warm climates around the world, including throughout Florida.
more_information: Fortnight lily is grown for being beautiful and nearly indestructible. It forms dense, upright clumps of stiff, grassy, fan-shaped leaves, out of which rise wiry stems carrying flat, six-parted flowers — white marked with yellow and violet — that look very much like a small iris, which is exactly what the plant is related to.

The common name comes from its bloom rhythm: flowers tend to appear in waves, often spaced about a fortnight (two weeks) apart, with each bloom lasting only a day or two but the show continuing over many months. The same flower stems are reused for years, so old stalks are best left uncut.
Its toughness is the selling point — it handles heat, drought, poor soil, and salt with little complaint, which is why it shows up in so many low-maintenance landscapes.
wildlife_value: The iris-like flowers attract bees and other pollinators. The dense, grassy clumps provide ground-level cover and shelter for small animals and insects.

blocks.label: Leaves

growing_conditions.note: very low-maintenance; leave old flower stems uncut, as they rebloom

edibility.detail: Not edible. Grown as an ornamental.

toxicity.people: Low toxicity. Iris-family plants can cause mild stomach upset if eaten, but it is not seriously poisonous.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. Iris-family plants (including the rhizomes) can cause mild vomiting or drooling in dogs if chewed; keep pets from grazing on it.

quick_hits: It looks like a white-flowered cousin of the orange Geiger tree — and it is, sharing the same rough, sandpapery leaves.
The showy white trumpet flowers have a soft yellow throat and bloom much of the year.
The small fruits that follow look like olives, giving it the name "Texas olive," though it is not a true olive.
It is notably cold-hardy for a tropical-looking tree, tolerating more frost than its orange Geiger relative.
species.origin: Native to southern Texas and northeastern Mexico. Grown as an ornamental flowering tree across the warm South, including Florida, and valued for its toughness and long bloom.
more_information: Texas olive — anacahuita in Mexico, where it is also native — is the white-flowered counterpart to the orange Geiger tree (Cordia sebestena), and the family resemblance is immediate: the same broad, stiff, sandpapery leaves, the same trumpet-shaped flowers in showy clusters — only here the blooms are crisp white with a yellow throat. It flowers heavily through much of the warm season.

The "olive" in Texas olive comes from the fruit, a small whitish drupe that resembles an olive but is unrelated to true olives. The fruits are edible in small amounts and have been used to make jellies and preserves, though eating many can be mildly intoxicating or laxative, and livestock that browse heavily on the foliage can be affected.
Its real advantage in the landscape is toughness: it handles heat, drought, and poor soil, and tolerates more cold than the tender orange Geiger, making it a hardier choice for a tropical look.
wildlife_value: The nectar-rich white flowers draw bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds over a long bloom season. The fruits are eaten by birds and other wildlife, and the dense canopy offers cover.

blocks.label: Leaves

toxicity.people: Low toxicity to people. Small amounts of fruit are harmless; large amounts may cause mild intoxication or a laxative effect.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be seriously harmful, though large amounts of fruit could upset a dog's stomach.

quick_hits: A Florida-native wildflower whose slender scarlet (sometimes pink or white) blooms are built for hummingbirds.
The tubular flowers fit a hummingbird's bill and tongue almost exactly — a partnership in plain sight.
It is a tireless self-seeder, popping up year after year and filling in gaps in a sunny bed.
A true sage (Salvia), it carries the family's faintly aromatic foliage.
species.origin: Native to the southeastern United States, including Florida, and south into Mexico and tropical America. A common wildflower of open woods, clearings, and disturbed ground, and a favorite in native and pollinator gardens.
more_information: Tropical sage is one of Florida's most rewarding native wildflowers for wildlife. Its loose spikes of slender, tubular flowers — usually vivid scarlet, sometimes pink, coral, or white — are shaped and colored for hummingbirds, which probe them for nectar, and they draw butterflies and bees as well.

It is also delightfully easy. The plant blooms over a long season, sets seed freely, and reseeds itself year after year, drifting through a sunny bed and filling in bare ground without becoming a nuisance. That self-sowing habit makes it a low-effort anchor for a pollinator planting.
As a true Salvia — the sage genus — it carries the family's lightly aromatic foliage, a soft herbal scent when the leaves are brushed.
wildlife_value: A top-tier pollinator plant: the tubular red flowers are a magnet for hummingbirds — especially the ruby-throated hummingbird, which migrates through Manatee County in spring and fall — and also feed butterflies and bees over a long bloom season. As a native that reseeds readily, it provides reliable, season-long nectar with little effort.

quick_hits: A tough, silvery-leaved Florida native that thrives right at the salty edge where most plants cannot.
Its cheerful yellow daisy flowers bloom much of the year along marsh and shoreline margins.
The thick, succulent gray-green leaves store water and shed salt — built-in coastal survival gear.
It spreads into dense, soil-holding colonies that help anchor shorelines against erosion.
species.origin: Native to the coastal southeastern United States, including Florida, and the Gulf and Caribbean coasts. A characteristic plant of salt marshes, mangrove edges, and brackish shorelines.
more_information: Sea ox-eye daisy is a salt-marsh specialist — a low, woody Florida native that grows where the ground is salty, wet, and punishing for ordinary plants. Its thick, fleshy, silvery-gray leaves are adapted to store water and cope with salt, giving the plant a frosted, coastal look.
Through much of the year it carries bright yellow, daisy-like flowers that stand out against the gray foliage and feed coastal pollinators. After flowering, the seed heads become small and burr-like.

Its real ecological job is holding ground. Sea ox-eye spreads by rhizomes into dense colonies that bind soil at the marsh and shoreline edge, buffering wave energy and resisting erosion — quiet, essential work in the transition zone between land and water.

There is no better plant to meet at this park: Palma Sola Botanical Park sits right beside Palma Sola Bay, and sea ox-eye is a plant of exactly that salt-marsh and shoreline edge — the same species holding the soil along the bay just outside the gates. It is a go-to plant for Tampa Bay–area shoreline restoration for that very reason.
wildlife_value: The yellow flowers provide nectar for bees, butterflies, and other coastal pollinators much of the year, and the seeds feed birds. Its dense colonies stabilize shorelines and offer cover for small marsh animals and insects.

blocks.label: Leaves

quick_hits: A spiny Florida-native shrub of salty shorelines whose bright red berries are edible — a wild cousin of the goji berry.
Native peoples and coastal communities ate the small red fruits, fresh or dried.
It belongs to the nightshade family, with small lavender star-flowers to match.
On the Texas coast the berries are a famous fall food for endangered whooping cranes — and here in Florida they feed shorebirds and coastal songbirds.
species.origin: Native to coastal salt marshes, flats, and shorelines of the southeastern United States, including Florida, and the Gulf and Atlantic coasts. A tough specialist of salty, sandy, often flooded ground.
more_information: Carolina wolfberry is a salt-tolerant native shrub that thrives along brackish shorelines and salt flats where few other plants persist. It is a true Lycium — the boxthorn genus — and a close relative of the goji berry (wolfberry) sold as a health food, which hints at its own edible fruit.

The small, juicy, bright red berries are edible and were eaten, fresh or dried, by Native peoples and coastal communities. As a member of the nightshade family it carries the family's small, star-shaped lavender flowers, and as with many nightshades, the ripe fruit is the part to eat while the rest of the plant is best left alone.

Ecologically the berries punch above their weight. On the Texas Gulf coast they are a documented key fall food for the endangered whooping cranes that winter there, whose arrival is timed to the berry crop. Florida's coast lies outside the whooping crane's wintering range, but here the same berries feed shorebirds and coastal songbirds — tying this unassuming shrub directly to coastal bird life.
wildlife_value: The small flowers feed bees and other pollinators, and the red berries are an important coastal bird food — most famously a key fall food for the endangered whooping cranes that winter on the Texas Gulf coast, and here a food for Florida's shorebirds and coastal songbirds. The spiny, scrambling growth offers protected cover and nesting sites.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A spiny, sprawling to scrambling native shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, lavender to white, star-shaped, nightshade-like.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, bright red, juicy berries.

blocks.label: Leaves

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A spiny coastal shrub with small fleshy gray-green leaves, lavender star-flowers, and bright red berries. The thorns plus succulent leaves on a shoreline mark it out.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · muck · brackish and salty soils · tolerates periodic flooding
growing_conditions.note: a true coastal specialist — excellent for salty shoreline edges

edibility.detail: The ripe red berries are edible — a native relative of the goji berry, eaten fresh or dried. As with other nightshades, eat only the ripe fruit.

toxicity.people: The ripe berries are edible. As a nightshade-family plant, the foliage and unripe fruit are best not eaten.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The ripe fruit is not known to be harmful; keep pets from grazing the foliage of nightshade-family plants.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida. Not invasive — a valuable coastal native.

quick_hits: A Florida-native waterlily whose fragrant white flowers float open on the pond surface — and follow the sun.
The blooms open in the morning and close by afternoon, repeating the cycle for several days.
That sweet fragrance is real and gives the plant its species name, odorata.
Many parts — buds, seeds, and roots — were traditional foods for Native peoples.
species.origin: Native to ponds, lakes, and slow waters across much of North America, including Florida. A common and characteristic plant of still freshwater throughout the state.
more_information: The American white waterlily is a true Florida native and one of the most recognizable plants of still freshwater: round, notched, floating leaves (lily pads) and fragrant, many-petaled white flowers resting on the surface, each with a sunburst of golden stamens at the center.

The flowers keep a daily schedule. They open in the morning, close again in the early afternoon, and repeat for several days before sinking below the surface to set seed underwater. The sweet scent — the reason for the name odorata — is strongest on freshly opened blooms.

Beyond beauty, it is a plant of genuine use and ecological weight. Its flower buds, seeds, and starchy rhizomes were eaten by Native peoples, and in the pond it shelters fish and invertebrates beneath its pads while feeding a range of wildlife above and below the water.
wildlife_value: The pads shade and shelter fish, frogs, and aquatic insects, while the flowers feed bees, beetles, and other pollinators. Seeds and foliage are eaten by waterfowl, and the whole stand supports a rich pond community above and below the surface.

blocks.label: Leaves

blocks.label: Reproduction
