reproduction.what_to_look_for: Notched, round floating pads and fragrant white surface flowers on a still pond. The morning-opening, afternoon-closing rhythm is part of the show.

size.spread: pads and flowers spreading across the water surface from rhizomes
size.water_depth: roots in mud under 1 to 5 feet of water

growing_conditions.water: still or slow fresh water, 1 to 5 feet deep, rooted in muck
growing_conditions.note: a native aquatic for ponds and pond margins; spreads by rhizome

edibility.detail: Edible historically — the flower buds, seeds, and starchy rhizomes were eaten by Native peoples. Not a casual food today.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people from the traditionally eaten parts.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida. Not invasive — a valued native aquatic (though it can spread vigorously in a small pond).

quick_hits: A sprawling Florida-native vine that runs across open sand just above the tide line, helping hold the dune in place.
Its rounded leaflets fold and turn through the day to manage the brutal beach sun.
The pink-purple pea flowers give way to thick, woody pods of hard seeds.
Those raw seeds are toxic — a reminder that "bean" does not always mean food.
species.origin: Native to tropical and subtropical beaches around the world, including Florida's coasts. A pioneering plant of open dunes and sandy shorelines just above the high-tide line.
more_information: Beach bean is a true coastal pioneer — a tough, salt- and sun-tolerant native legume that sends long runners sprawling across bare sand at the top of the beach. Those runners root as they go, knitting the loose sand together and helping build and stabilize the foredune, quiet but important shoreline work.

Like many beach plants, its rounded leaflets fold and angle through the day, a built-in way to limit water loss and sunburn in a punishing environment. In season it carries pretty pink to purple pea-family flowers, followed by thick, woody pods filled with hard seeds.

The pods come with a caveat. The raw seeds contain toxic compounds (including canavanine) and are not safe to eat raw; only careful, traditional processing makes them usable as food in some cultures. It is a plant to admire on the dune, not to snack from.
wildlife_value: The pea flowers attract bees and other pollinators, and as a legume the plant fixes nitrogen, enriching poor beach sand. Its sprawling runners stabilize dunes and provide low cover for small coastal animals and insects.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A sprawling, trailing native vine of open sand.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Pink to rose-purple pea flowers in clusters.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Thick, woody, oblong pods containing hard seeds.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Compound with three rounded leaflets that fold and angle through the day.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Long runners with three-part rounded leaves trailing across beach sand, with pink-purple pea flowers and thick pods. Its place just above the tide line is itself a clue.

size.spread: runners to 10 to 20 feet or more

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: pure sand · salty soils · very well-drained
growing_conditions.note: a dune pioneer — ideal for open, sandy, salty sites

edibility.detail: Not a casual food — the raw seeds are toxic and need careful traditional processing to be edible. Best treated as a non-food dune plant.

toxicity.people: The raw seeds are toxic (they contain canavanine and related compounds) and should not be eaten raw. The growing plant is otherwise low-risk.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. The raw seeds and pods are toxic and could harm a dog that chews them; keep pets from eating the pods.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida. Not invasive — a beneficial dune stabilizer.

quick_hits: The little white-and-yellow daisy that pops up everywhere is one of Florida's most valuable nectar plants for bees and butterflies.
Its seeds are the flat black "sticktights" that hitchhike home on your socks and your dog.
Despite its weedy reputation, the young leaves and flowers are edible — used as a cooked green and herbal tea.
It blooms nearly year-round, feeding pollinators when little else is flowering.
species.origin: Native to Florida and the warm Americas. An abundant wildflower of roadsides, fields, clearings, and disturbed ground throughout the state, often dismissed as a weed.
more_information: White beggarticks — "Spanish needles" to many — is one of those plants whose weedy reputation hides real worth. Its small white-rayed, yellow-centered daisies bloom nearly year-round, and they are a powerhouse nectar and pollen source. Florida studies repeatedly rank it among the very top plants for honeybees, native bees, and butterflies, precisely because it flowers when so little else does.

The nickname comes from the seeds: flat, narrow, black, and tipped with tiny barbs that latch onto fur, feathers, and clothing — the familiar "sticktights" or "beggar's ticks" you pick off after a walk. It is the plant's highly effective hitchhiking strategy for getting its seeds moved around.

It is also a genuine wild edible: the young leaves and flowers are eaten as a cooked green and steeped as an herbal tea, with a long history of folk medicinal use. Weed, wildflower, pollinator buffet, and potherb all at once.
wildlife_value: One of Florida's most important nectar and pollen plants — consistently ranked among the top sources for honeybees, native bees, and butterflies, and a larval host for several butterflies and moths, including the dainty sulphur. It feeds pollinators nearly year-round, including in lean seasons when little else blooms.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A fast-growing, branching native annual or short-lived perennial wildflower.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small daisies with white rays and yellow centers, blooming nearly year-round.

blocks.label: Fruit/Seeds
blocks.text: Flat, narrow, black seeds tipped with barbs ("sticktights") that cling to fur and clothing.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Toothed, often divided into leaflets, medium green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Small white-and-yellow daisies on a weedy, branching plant — and the barbed black seeds that stick to your clothes are the unmistakable confirmation.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · tolerates poor and disturbed soil
growing_conditions.note: reseeds aggressively — welcome for pollinators, but pull where you do not want it

edibility.detail: Edible — the young leaves and flowers are eaten as a cooked green and steeped as an herbal tea. A genuine wild edible.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people. Young leaves and flowers are eaten; long folk-medicinal history.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida. Not invasive (though it is weedy and reseeds freely). A top pollinator plant often left as a 'good weed.'

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #365602487 (greg_bratlee); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: A handsome vine with glossy leaves and large funnel-shaped lavender-pink flowers — and a serious dark side.
Every part runs with milky sap that is highly poisonous, packed with heart-stopping toxins.
It is one of the world's worst invasive weeds, smothering native vegetation where it escapes.
The park keeps it as a teaching example: beautiful, useful-looking, and exactly the kind of plant not to turn loose.
species.origin: Native to Madagascar. Introduced widely as an ornamental and rubber-source plant, it has become a damaging invasive weed in parts of Australia, the Pacific, and elsewhere, and is treated as a plant of concern where it escapes.
more_information: Madagascar rubbervine is a cautionary tale wrapped in an attractive package. The vine is genuinely handsome — glossy paired leaves and large, funnel-shaped flowers in soft lavender-pink — and it was spread around the warm world as an ornamental and as a potential rubber source, thanks to its abundant latex.

That latex is the problem on two fronts. First, it is highly toxic: the milky sap contains cardiac glycosides — heart-affecting poisons — and all parts of the plant are dangerous to people and livestock if eaten, with the sap also an irritant. Second, the vine is aggressively invasive where it escapes, climbing over and smothering native trees and shrubs; in parts of Australia, rubber vines (this species together with its close relative Cryptostegia grandiflora, which are easily confused and hybridize) became some of the most serious environmental weeds on the continent.
The park keeps it deliberately, as a teaching plant — a vivid lesson that "pretty and useful" can still mean "poisonous and invasive," and a reason to think hard before planting an unfamiliar exotic.
wildlife_value: Wildlife value is low and outweighed by its hazards. The flowers offer some nectar, but the plant's toxicity and its tendency to smother native vegetation make it a net negative for local wildlife.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A vigorous woody, climbing or scrambling evergreen vine with milky sap.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Large, funnel-shaped, lavender-pink (sometimes paler), in clusters.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Paired, horn-like pods that split to release silky-tufted seeds carried on the wind.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, dark green, oval, opposite, exuding white latex when broken.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A glossy-leaved climbing vine with funnel-shaped lavender-pink flowers and milky sap. Paired horn-like seed pods confirm it — and signal a plant to control, not spread.

size.height: climbs 10 to 30 feet into supporting vegetation

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · tolerant of poor soils
growing_conditions.note: aggressive and toxic — kept here only as a managed teaching specimen

edibility.detail: Not edible — poisonous. No part should be eaten.

toxicity.people: Highly toxic if ingested, and the milky sap irritates skin and eyes. Contains cardiac glycosides that affect the heart. All parts are dangerous; do not eat any part and avoid the sap.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs. The cardiac glycosides in the sap and foliage can cause vomiting, heart rhythm problems, and serious illness. Keep pets away.

invasive.notes: A recognized invasive of serious concern — one of the world's worst environmental weeds where it escapes (notoriously in Australia). Grown here only as a contained teaching example; not a plant to propagate or release.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #363461727 (joanne_walker); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: A sprawling shrub that lights up in fall with bright yellow flowers — handsome, and a recognized Florida invasive.
It blooms around the holidays, which is how it earned the name Christmas senna.
Long, dangling bean pods follow the flowers, scattering seeds that sprout readily and let it spread.
Like its relatives, it is a butterfly host — sulphur caterpillars feed on it — which complicates an otherwise weedy reputation.
species.origin: Native to South America. Introduced as an ornamental for its bright fall bloom, it has escaped cultivation and naturalized in Florida, where it is listed as an invasive species.
more_information: Christmas senna is a fast, sprawling, semi-woody shrub grown for its showy display of bright yellow, cup-shaped flowers, which peak in fall and early winter — the source of the holiday name. After flowering come long, hanging, cylindrical seed pods typical of the senna group.

Those seeds are the catch. They germinate readily, and the plant has escaped gardens to naturalize across Florida, where it is recognized as an invasive (FISC Category II) that crowds into natural and disturbed areas. The park includes it partly as a teaching example of an attractive ornamental that does not stay put.

There is a genuine ecological twist, though: like other sennas and cassias, it serves as a larval host for sulphur butterflies, whose caterpillars feed on the foliage — so even a plant being managed as a weed can carry real value for pollinators, a nuance worth posting honestly.
wildlife_value: A larval host for sulphur butterflies (such as cloudless and orange-barred sulphurs), whose caterpillars feed on the leaves, and the flowers offer nectar and pollen to bees. That butterfly-host value is set against its status as an invasive that displaces native plants.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A fast-growing, sprawling, semi-woody shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Bright yellow, cup-shaped, in clusters, peaking in fall and early winter.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Long, hanging, cylindrical pods containing many readily sprouting seeds.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnately compound with several pairs of oval leaflets.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A loose, sprawling shrub covered in bright yellow flowers in fall, followed by long dangling pods. The pods plus the autumn bloom mark it — and flag a plant that spreads.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · tolerant of poor and disturbed soils
growing_conditions.note: spreads readily by seed — manage to prevent escape into natural areas

edibility.detail: Not edible. The pods and seeds are not for eating.

toxicity.people: Mild toxicity. The pods and seeds have a laxative (cathartic) effect and can cause stomach upset if eaten; not a serious poison but not a food.
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. Senna species can cause vomiting and diarrhea in dogs (cathartic effect); keep pets from eating the pods and seeds.

invasive.notes: Listed as an invasive in Florida (FISC Category II). It escapes cultivation and naturalizes; grown here partly as a teaching example. Manage to prevent spread; do not plant where it can escape.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #368046717 (frankymca); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: A sprawling succulent groundcover with fat, triangular, water-storing leaves that hug the ground.
It opens large, satiny, daisy-like flowers in pink to yellow that track the sun across the sky.
The fig-shaped fruit is edible — eaten fresh or made into a tart jam in its native South Africa, the "sour fig."
A fierce coastal colonizer, it is a notorious invasive in places like California and the Mediterranean — handsome, but to watch.
species.origin: Native to coastal South Africa. Planted worldwide for erosion control and as an ornamental succulent groundcover, it has become a serious invasive on coastlines in California, the Mediterranean, and Australia.
more_information: Sea fig (Carpobrotus) is an iceplant relative built for the coast: long, trailing stems set with thick, three-sided, succulent leaves that store water and let it carpet harsh, sandy, salty ground. It opens large, glossy, many-petaled, daisy-like flowers in shades of pink, magenta, or yellow that follow the sun through the day.

Its common name comes from the fruit. In its native South Africa, the fleshy, fig-shaped fruit of the true sour fig is eaten fresh or cooked into a tangy jam, and the leaf juice has folk-remedy uses — a genuinely useful plant in its homeland.

Away from home, though, it has a reputation. The same vigor that makes it a fine sand-binder makes it a smothering invasive on coastlines in California, the Mediterranean basin, and Australia, where it blankets and outcompetes native dune plants. The park's plant is identified at the genus level; it is worth admiring with that cautionary history in mind.
wildlife_value: The large flowers offer nectar and pollen to bees and other insects, and the fruit is eaten by some animals in its native range. Against this, where it naturalizes it tends to smother native groundcovers, reducing overall habitat value.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A trailing, mat-forming succulent groundcover.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Large, glossy, many-petaled, daisy-like, in pink, magenta, or yellow; opening with the sun.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Fleshy, fig-shaped; edible in the true sour fig.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Thick, three-sided (triangular in cross-section), succulent, gray-green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A flat-spreading mat of fat, triangular succulent leaves studded with large satiny daisy flowers. The three-sided leaves and fig-like fruit are the tells.

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · salty soils · very well-drained
growing_conditions.note: vigorous — can overrun an area; manage to prevent spread

edibility.detail: The fig-like fruit of the true sour fig is edible — eaten fresh or made into a tart jam in its native South Africa.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people; the ripe fruit is eaten in its native range.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: A notorious coastal invasive in California, the Mediterranean, and Australia, where it smothers native dune plants. Less aggressive in Florida, but vigorous — plant thoughtfully and contain it.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #348376147 (coleguyton); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review. Genus-level ID — refine to species when possible.

quick_hits: A slender orange-and-black bug with long legs, prowling leaves and stems, is a milkweed assassin bug — a fierce hunter of other insects.
It is firmly on the gardener's side. It hunts down aphids, caterpillars, and many pest insects, helping keep gardens in balance.
It kills with a stab. A sharp curved beak injects venom that paralyzes prey and dissolves its insides, which the assassin bug then drinks.
A word of caution: that same beak can deliver a sharp, briefly painful jab if the bug is handled or trapped against skin.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the warmer Americas and common across the southern United States. In Florida it is widespread in gardens, fields, and brushy areas through the warm season.
more_information: The milkweed assassin bug is a small predator with an outsized appetite for pests. Bright orange and black with long, spindly legs, it stalks foliage in search of aphids, caterpillars, beetle larvae, and other soft-bodied insects, making it a welcome presence in most gardens.

Its hunting tool is a curved, needle-like beak (a rostrum). The bug grabs prey with its sticky front legs, drives in the beak, and injects saliva that both paralyzes the victim and liquefies its tissues, then drinks the meal — efficient, if grim.

The one caution is the same beak. This is a predator built to pierce, and a carelessly handled or pinned assassin bug can stab in self-defense, delivering a sharp, briefly painful jab. Watch it work, but let it be.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Bright reddish-orange and black, with long thin legs and antennae.

blocks.label: Beak
blocks.text: A short, curved piercing beak tucked under the head.

blocks.label: Body
blocks.text: Slender and elongated, with a narrow "neck."

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Moves deliberately over leaves and stems, hunting.

identification.what_to_look_for: A leggy orange-and-black bug creeping over garden foliage, often near aphid clusters or other small insects. The slim build and curved beak mark it as an assassin bug rather than a harmless look-alike.

species.diet: Other insects — aphids, caterpillars, beetle larvae, flies, and various soft-bodied pests — caught and drained through its piercing beak.
species.behavior: Active by day, hunting slowly and patiently across foliage. Ambushes and grabs prey with sticky forelegs. Solitary and not aggressive toward people unless handled.
species.ecological_role: A beneficial predator that helps control aphids and other plant pests, a natural ally in keeping garden insect populations in check.
species.plant_connections: Hunts across garden and wild foliage of all kinds, drawn to plants where pest insects gather.

size.lifespan: adults live several weeks to a few months.

species.habitat: Gardens, field edges, and brushy areas with plenty of plants and insect prey.
species.where_to_look: On foliage throughout the garden beds, often near clusters of aphids or other small insects. Look closely and let it hunt undisturbed.
species.when_to_see: Most active in the warm season, spring through fall.

danger.people: Harmless if left alone, but a handled or trapped assassin bug can stab with its beak, delivering a sharp, briefly painful jab. Do not pick it up.
danger.pets: Not a real danger to pets, which generally leave it alone.

interaction.guidance: Look, do not touch. Enjoy watching it hunt, but do not handle it — that curved beak is for self-defense as well as prey.

invasive.notes: Native to the Americas and to Florida. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Not a conservation concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A fast, dark butterfly with an iridescent blue-green body and two long tails trailing from its hindwings is a long-tailed skipper.
Skippers are the fighter jets of the butterfly world — small, stout, and quick, with a darting, hard-to-follow flight.
That blue-green shimmer on its body and wing bases catches the light beautifully when it pauses to nectar.
Its caterpillar has a second career as the "bean leafroller," folding and stitching bean and pea leaves into shelters — occasionally to a gardener's annoyance.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States and south through the American tropics. In Florida it is a common butterfly of gardens, field edges, and weedy open ground.
more_information: The long-tailed skipper belongs to the skipper family, a group of small, powerfully built butterflies named for their quick, skipping flight. This one stands out for the pair of long tails on its hindwings and the iridescent blue-green sheen across its furry body and inner wings.
Adults are eager flower-visitors, uncoiling a long proboscis to drink nectar and pollinating as they go. Their flight is fast and erratic, which makes them easy to overlook and hard to follow.

The caterpillar leads a different life. Known as the bean leafroller, it cuts and folds the leaves of beans, peas, and related legumes into rolled shelters held with silk, feeding from inside. In a vegetable garden it can be a minor pest, but it is the same insect that becomes the shimmering adult skipping among the flowers.

blocks.label: Body and wing bases
blocks.text: Iridescent blue-green over a dark brown ground color.

blocks.label: Hindwings
blocks.text: Each drawn out into a long tail.

blocks.label: Underside
blocks.text: Brown with darker bands.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Fast, low, and darting.

identification.what_to_look_for: A dark, quick butterfly with a blue-green sheen and two long hindwing tails, nectaring at flowers or zipping low over weedy ground. The long tails and shimmer separate it from other skippers.

species.diet: Adults sip nectar from many flowers. Caterpillars eat the leaves of beans, peas, and other legumes.
species.behavior: Active by day, with a fast, darting flight low over vegetation. Visits flowers frequently and basks with wings partly open. Caterpillars build rolled-leaf shelters on host plants.
species.ecological_role: A pollinator as an adult and, as a caterpillar, a leaf-feeder on legumes — part of the everyday garden food web and prey for birds and other insects.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillar host plants are legumes — beans, peas, and wild relatives; adults nectar at a wide range of flowers.

size.lifespan: adults live a couple of weeks.

species.habitat: Gardens, field and woodland edges, weedy lots, and other open, flowery places, especially near legume plants.
species.where_to_look: Around the flower beds and weedy edges, nectaring or darting low. Watch for the long-tailed silhouette and blue-green flash.
species.when_to_see: A warm-season butterfly, common from spring through fall.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Avoid handling its delicate wings; planting nectar flowers is the best way to attract it.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the southeastern U.S. Not invasive (though its bean-leafroller caterpillar can be a minor garden nuisance).

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #17217419 (ktreffinger); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: Both flash blue-green, but the long-tailed skipper is smaller and trails two long hindwing tails; the mangrove skipper is larger, tail-less, with bolder neon-blue streaks.

quick_hits: A tiny pale-blue butterfly, barely bigger than a thumbnail, flickering low over the flower beds is a cassius blue — one of Florida's smallest and most common butterflies.
Males shine pale blue above; females are duller, with more dusky gray and brown.
Flip to the underside and you find a delicate maze of fine gray-and-white bands, with one or two small black-and-orange eyespots near the tail.
Those eyespots, paired with tiny hindwing tails the butterfly wiggles, create a "false head" that fools a predator into striking the wrong end.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States, the Caribbean, and south into the American tropics. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident of gardens, parks, and weedy open ground.
more_information: The cassius blue is a member of the gossamer-wing family — small, fragile butterflies often no bigger than a fingernail. Males are a soft, pale blue above; females wear more gray and brown, so the two can look like different butterflies at a glance.

Turn one over and the underside tells the real story: a fine pattern of gray-and-white bands and a couple of small black-and-orange eyespots near the rear edge of the hindwing. Combined with tiny tails that the resting butterfly slowly wiggles, those spots mimic a head and antennae at the wrong end of the body. A bird aiming for the "head" gets a harmless beakful of wingtip, and the butterfly escapes.

It is an easy butterfly to host. Its caterpillars feed on the buds and flowers of legumes and on plumbago, both common in Florida landscaping, which is part of why the cassius blue is such a constant presence in gardens.

blocks.label: Upperside
blocks.text: Males pale blue; females duller blue-gray with brown.

blocks.label: Underside
blocks.text: White to pale gray with fine gray bands and one or two small black-and-orange eyespots near the tail.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Very small, wingspan around 1 in (2.5 cm).

blocks.label: Tails
blocks.text: Tiny threadlike tails on the hindwings.

identification.what_to_look_for: A very small, pale powder-blue butterfly fluttering low around flowers. The fine-banded underside with little eyespots near a tiny tail confirms a cassius blue.

species.diet: Adults sip nectar from small flowers. Caterpillars feed on the flowers and buds of legumes and on plumbago.
species.behavior: Active by day, with a low, fluttery flight close to the ground and flowers. Often perches with wings closed, slowly moving the hindwings so the false-head eyespots and tails twitch.
species.ecological_role: A small pollinator of low flowers and prey for spiders, birds, and other insects — a minor but constant thread in the garden food web.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillar host plants include legumes (such as peas and wild beans) and plumbago (leadwort) — common garden plants, which keeps this butterfly close to home.

size.lifespan: adults live a couple of weeks.

species.habitat: Gardens, parks, weedy fields, and disturbed open ground with low flowers and legume or plumbago host plants.
species.where_to_look: Low around the flower beds and any plumbago plantings, fluttering close to the blooms. Look small — it is easy to miss.
species.when_to_see: Year-round in Florida's warm climate, most numerous in the warmer months.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Avoid handling its delicate wings; low-growing nectar flowers and plumbago will keep it around.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the southeastern U.S. Not invasive.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #328293707 (katiet5); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

quick_hits: It looks exactly like an American crow — slightly smaller, slightly glossier — and the only reliable way to tell is the voice: a short, nasal 'uh-uh' instead of the familiar 'caw.'
It is the crow of coastal and riverside Florida, far more common near water than its larger cousin.
An opportunist through and through — fish, garbage, eggs stolen from other birds' nests, fast food, whatever is available.
If you hear a crow near the ponds and it sounds a little nasally and uncertain, that is probably the fish crow.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern and mid-Atlantic United States. Year-round resident along the Florida coast and waterways.
more_information: The fish crow is one of those birds that exists in the shadow of a more famous relative. It is slightly smaller than the American crow and nearly identical in appearance, so most people never realize there are two species.
The voice is the key — a short, somewhat nasal, two-note call often described as 'uh-uh' or 'uh-oh,' lacking the full-throated 'caw' of the American crow.
Like all corvids it is intelligent and social, foraging in groups and quickly learning to exploit human food sources.

blocks.label: Voice
blocks.text: Short, nasal 'uh-uh' — distinctly different from the American crow's full 'caw.'

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Slightly smaller than an American crow, but the difference is subtle.

blocks.label: Habitat
blocks.text: Strongly tied to water — coast, rivers, ponds.

blocks.label: Behavior
blocks.text: Often in small groups near water or open areas.

identification.what_to_look_for: A crow near water that gives a short, nasal 'uh-uh' instead of a full 'caw.' If you are near the coast or a pond in Florida and hear a crow, the odds favor fish crow.

species.diet: Fish, crustaceans, insects, eggs, fruit, garbage — a true generalist.
species.behavior: Forages on the ground, in shallow water, and in trees. Robs nests. Gathers in communal roosts.
species.sounds: A short, nasal 'uh-uh' or 'uh-oh' — the single best way to separate it from the American crow.
species.ecological_role: Scavenger and nest predator; part of the coastal food web.
species.plant_connections: Eats some fruit and berries; forages around many tree species.
species.habitat: Coastal areas, rivers, ponds, parking lots, and open ground near water.
species.where_to_look: Near the ponds or anywhere open — listen for the nasal call.
species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-19 from iNat observations export (June 2026 batch). Content drafted; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

quick_hits: The only breeding hummingbird in the eastern United States — a tiny, jewel-green bird that hovers at flowers and feeders, beating its wings about 53 times per second.
The male's throat blazes iridescent ruby-red in the right light — turn your head and it looks black. The color is structural, not pigmented, created by microscopic platelets in the feathers.
Every fall, many cross the Gulf of Mexico nonstop — roughly 500 miles of open water with nowhere to land.
It weighs about as much as a penny, yet it is fiercely territorial and will chase birds many times its size away from a flower patch.
species.range_and_origin: Native to eastern North America. Breeds across the eastern U.S. and southern Canada; winters in Central America. Present in Florida mainly during migration and winter, though some breed in the northern part of the state.
more_information: The ruby-throated hummingbird is the default hummingbird of the eastern United States — the only species that regularly breeds east of the Mississippi. Its tiny size, hovering flight, and iridescent plumage make it one of the most charismatic visitors to any garden.

To fuel its metabolism, it visits hundreds of flowers per day and also eats tiny insects and spiders for protein. Its heart beats over 1,200 times per minute in flight, and it enters a torpor-like state overnight to conserve energy.
In the Bradenton area it is most likely during fall and spring migration, with some wintering birds.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Tiny — about 3 inches long, weighing roughly a tenth of an ounce.

blocks.label: Male
blocks.text: Iridescent green above, white below, with a brilliant ruby-red throat (gorget) that can look black.

blocks.label: Female
blocks.text: Green above, white below, no red throat.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Hovers in place, wings beating so fast they blur.

identification.what_to_look_for: A tiny hovering bird at flowers or feeders. Males flash a ruby-red throat in the right light. Listen for the humming of the wings.

species.diet: Nectar from flowers and feeders; tiny insects and spiders for protein.
species.behavior: Hovers to feed. Fiercely territorial — chases other hummingbirds and larger birds from flower patches. Incredibly fast and agile.
species.sounds: A rapid, chittering series of notes; the hum of the wings is often the first clue.
species.ecological_role: Pollinator of tubular flowers; insect predator. A key player in the pollination of many native wildflowers.
species.plant_connections: Visits many native and garden flowers, especially tubular red, orange, and pink blooms.

size.lifespan: commonly 3 to 5 years, occasionally much longer.

species.habitat: Gardens, woodland edges, and flowery areas during migration.
species.where_to_look: Around the flower beds, especially tubular blooms. Listen for the wing hum.
species.when_to_see: Most likely during fall and spring migration; some winter here. A seasonal visitor.

seasonality.note: Breeds farther north; passes through and winters here in smaller numbers.

quick_hits: A small, dark rabbit with short ears and a tiny brownish tail — not the long-eared cottontail most people picture when they hear 'rabbit.'
It is one of the very few rabbits that swims, and it does so willingly — wading into water to escape predators or to reach better feeding areas.
Unlike cottontails, the marsh rabbit's tail is not white and fluffy — it is small, brown, and inconspicuous.
It is a true Florida native, tied to the state's wetlands, marshes, and dense, damp vegetation.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States from Virginia to Florida. Year-round resident in Florida's wetlands.
more_information: The marsh rabbit is the rabbit of Florida's damp places — swamps, pond edges, marshes, and dense wet thickets. It is smaller and darker than the eastern cottontail, with proportionally shorter ears and a dingy brown tail instead of the cottontail's white powder puff.

Its willingness to swim sets it apart from most rabbits. When threatened, it may plunge into water and swim with only its nose above the surface, or sit motionless in shallow water among emergent vegetation.
It is most active at dawn and dusk, feeding on grasses, sedges, and aquatic plants.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Small — smaller than a cottontail, about 14 to 16 inches.

blocks.label: Ears
blocks.text: Short for a rabbit.

blocks.label: Tail
blocks.text: Small, brown — not the white 'cotton ball' of a cottontail.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Dark brown to blackish-brown, darker than a cottontail.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small, dark rabbit near water with short ears and no white tail. If it runs toward water instead of away, it is a marsh rabbit.

species.diet: Grasses, sedges, aquatic plants, and other low vegetation — a strict herbivore.
species.behavior: Crepuscular — most active at dawn and dusk. Swims readily. Freezes when threatened before bolting.
species.sounds: Generally silent; may let out a high squeal if captured.
species.ecological_role: Herbivore and prey animal — a food source for hawks, owls, bobcats, and snakes.
species.plant_connections: Feeds on grasses, sedges, and wetland plants.

size.lifespan: commonly 1 to 4 years in the wild.

species.habitat: Marshes, wet thickets, pond edges, and dense vegetation near water.
species.where_to_look: Along pond edges and in dense low vegetation, especially at dawn or dusk.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, most visible at dawn and dusk.

quick_hits: A small, fast butterfly checkered in gray, white, and blue — darting low over open ground and landing with wings spread flat.
Males develop a dusting of blue-gray hairs on the body that gives them a frosty look.
It is a skipper — the 'fighter jet' family of butterflies — small-bodied, thick-muscled, and quick.
Common in disturbed, weedy, open areas — exactly the kind of habitat a botanical park provides.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern U.S., Caribbean, and tropical Americas. Year-round resident in southern Florida.
more_information: The tropical checkered-skipper is one of the more recognizable skippers thanks to its bold checkered wing pattern of gray, white, and dark marks. Males get an additional dusting of blue-gray hairs that gives them a distinctive frosty appearance.
It is a butterfly of disturbed, open ground — roadsides, gardens, weedy lots, and park edges — where its caterpillar host plants (mallows) grow.
Like other skippers, it flies fast and low, and rests with wings held open and flat rather than folded up.

blocks.label: Pattern
blocks.text: Bold checkered gray, white, and dark pattern on the wings.

blocks.label: Male
blocks.text: Frosty blue-gray hair on the body and wing bases.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Fast, low, darting — typical skipper.

blocks.label: Posture
blocks.text: Rests with wings spread flat.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small, fast, checkered gray-and-white butterfly resting flat on the ground or low vegetation in an open, weedy area.

species.diet: Adults sip nectar from small flowers. Caterpillars feed on plants in the mallow family.
species.behavior: Fast, darting flight close to the ground. Basks with wings open. Males patrol low over open ground.
species.ecological_role: Pollinator of low flowers; caterpillars feed on mallow-family plants.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillar host plants are mallows (Malvaceae). Adults nectar at many low flowers.

size.lifespan: adults live a couple of weeks.

species.habitat: Disturbed open ground, gardens, roadsides, weedy lots.
species.where_to_look: Open, sunny, weedy areas and garden edges. Watch for the fast, low flight.
species.when_to_see: Year-round in southern Florida, most numerous in warm months.
